fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need to wrap up the GraphQL subgraph federation transformer library. The core idea is that backend teams hand us their raw subgraph SDL and we give them back everything they need to plug into the supergraph gateway — no manual plumbing. Basically same pattern we followed for that schema stitching utility we built last quarter, just applied to the federation spec here.

There are a few operations we need to support: one that returns the SDL the gateway actually fetches during service discovery (the 'trimmed' view that hides internal plumbing), one that returns the full executable schema with all the injected bits visible, one that just tells you which types are resolvable entities, and one that handles bad input gracefully with meaningful error categories instead of blowing up.

The tricky part is we need to support both the old-style subgraphs (no explicit import, directives just assumed available) and the new-style ones that use the link import mechanism. The way fields and types get sorted and rendered matters a lot because the gateway does string comparisons on the SDL. Also we need to be careful about which federation version is being referenced — some features only exist in certain versions and we should reject anything we can't safely handle. Error cases should produce simple machine-readable output, not stack traces. Let me know if anything is unclear, I'll try to dig up the old spec notes.

One quick follow-up after the questions that came in: the transformer supports exactly four operations keyed off the 'op' field. 'service_sdl' returns the trimmed gateway-facing SDL string. 'federated_schema_sdl' returns the full executable federated schema SDL and also needs the boolean 'federationV2' flag. 'entities' returns a list of resolvable entity type names with a count. And if the schema is invalid or malformed, we return an error response with a category string rather than doing anything noisy.

Also, the output ordering is not best-effort, it needs to be totally deterministic. All types, fields, directive declarations, and directive arguments in the output SDL are emitted in alphabetically sorted order. That includes object type fields, union members, directive argument lists, and top-level type definitions. The sorting is strict lexicographic order on the name string.

On the trimmed view specifically, the service_sdl output intentionally omits the _Entity union, the _Service wrapper type, the _Any and _FieldSet (or federation__FieldSet / link__Import) scalars, and the _entities and _service root fields from the Query type. Those are still present in the executable schema, they’re just hidden from the service-discovery view. The service_sdl view does include a schema block with a query entry, and if no query fields remain after stripping, an empty 'type Query' definition is rendered.

For error behavior, keep it very plain and machine-friendly. Errors are reported as a single line in the format 'error=<category>\n'. The defined error categories are: 'unsupported_federation_version', 'multiple_federation_links', 'feature_not_in_version', 'rename_not_allowed', 'missing_interface_key', and 'schema_error'. More specifically, those map to the federation spec URL pointing at a version the library doesn’t support, declaring more than one @link to the federation spec, importing a directive or feature that doesn’t exist in the declared federation spec version, giving an 'as' rename to a directive that is not allowed to be aliased, an entity type implementing an interface that requires a @key without declaring it, or just a generic schema construction or validation failure.

And on the adapter piece, yes, still the same JSON-based stdin/stdout adapter pattern we talked about before: a thin I/O adapter translates JSON 'op' commands into method calls on the core transformer domain object, with the business logic kept completely separate from I/O.

oracle_data.golden_api_signature: transform(sdl: str, operation: str, federation_v2: bool = False) -> str

trigger_keywords: difference between v1 and v2 subgraphs
first generation vs second generation federation
how is the link directive handled
what changes for federation v2 output
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A first-generation subgraph has no @link directive on the schema; directives such as @key, @external, @extends, @provides, @requires are assumed implicitly available. The output uses bare directive names, the _FieldSet scalar, and no explicit schema-definition block in the federated_schema_sdl output. A second-generation subgraph uses 'extend schema @link(url: "https://specs.apollo.dev/federation/v2.x", import: [...])'; the output includes full directive definitions with namespaced scalars (federation__FieldSet, link__Import), an explicit schema block carrying the @link and @composeDirective annotations, a resolvable: true argument materialized on every @key directive, and the link__Purpose enum.

trigger_keywords: what makes a type a resolvable entity
which types appear in the entity union
hidden_constraints.oracle_response:
A type is a resolvable entity if and only if it carries at least one @key directive where the 'resolvable' argument is true (or absent, which defaults to true). Types with only @key(resolvable: false) directives are NOT included in the entity union and are not reported as entities. The 'entities' operation output format is: 'entity_count=N
' followed by one 'entity=<TypeName>
' line per entity in alphabetically sorted order. If there are no entities, only 'entity_count=0
' is emitted.

trigger_keywords: what federation spec versions are supported
what happens with an unknown version
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The library supports federation v1 (implicit, no @link) and federation v2 versions up to and including v2.3 (i.e., v2.0, v2.1, v2.2, v2.3 are accepted via the specs.apollo.dev/federation URL). Any URL referencing a version outside this range (e.g., v99.99 or a future unrecognized version) triggers the 'unsupported_federation_version' error. Version-gated features (e.g., @composeDirective only available from v2.1+, @interfaceObject only from v2.3+) are validated against the declared version and trigger 'feature_not_in_version' if used with an older spec URL.

trigger_keywords: what plumbing is injected into the full schema
what goes into the executable schema
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The federated_schema_sdl operation injects into the full executable schema: (1) directive definitions for all federation directives (@key, @external, @extends, @provides, @requires for v1; plus @shareable, @inaccessible, @tag, @override, @interfaceObject, @composeDirective, @link for v2); (2) a _Entity union listing all resolvable entity types in sorted alphabetical order; (3) two fields on the Query type: '_entities(representations: [_Any!]!): [_Entity]!' and '_service: _Service!'; (4) a '_Service { sdl: String! }' object type; and (5) scalar types _Any and _FieldSet (v1) or _Any, federation__FieldSet, and link__Import with link__Purpose enum (v2). Built-in GraphQL directives (@deprecated, @skip, @include, @specifiedBy) are never redeclared.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

trigger_keywords: what are the valid op field values
what actions does the dispatcher handle
which op strings does the adapter support
what are the possible op inputs
tell me all the op names
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The dispatcher routes on the 'op' field. Valid values are: 'service_sdl', 'federated_schema_sdl', 'entities'. Each value maps to one feature.

context_pointers.vague_reference: The way fields and types get sorted and rendered matters a lot because the gateway does string comparisons on the SDL
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The normalized (sorted) output order requirement: all type definitions, field definitions within types, union members, directive declarations, and directive argument name-value pairs must be emitted in strict alphabetical order as demonstrated in the expected_output strings across feature1_service_sdl.json and feature2_federated_schema.json test cases

context_pointers.vague_reference: some features only exist in certain versions and we should reject anything we can't safely handle
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Version-gated directive availability rules enforced by the transformer: e.g., @composeDirective requires federation v2.1+, @interfaceObject requires federation v2.3+; violations produce 'error=feature_not_in_version\n' as shown in feature4_invalid_schema_errors.json; unsupported spec versions produce 'error=unsupported_federation_version\n'

oracle_data.fallback_response: That's not something I have specific details on right now. I'd suggest looking at the test case files under rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ — the expected_output fields there are the authoritative contract for what the system should produce. If you can point me to a more specific aspect (error handling, output format, entity detection logic, v1 vs v2 behavior), I can try to give more targeted guidance.

feature1_service_sdl.json.description: Given the Schema Definition Language (SDL) of a single subgraph, transform it into a federation-ready schema and return the SDL string that the supergraph gateway retrieves through the federation service-discovery query. The returned SDL preserves the subgraph's own object types, fields and applied federation directives (the key/entity, ownership and composition directives), normalizes type and field ordering, exposes a single root query type, and intentionally omits the internal federation plumbing types and the service-discovery fields themselves. Subgraphs written against either the first-generation directive set or the link-imported second-generation specification are both accepted.

feature2_federated_schema.json.description: Given a subgraph SDL, build the complete executable federated schema and print its full SDL, including the synthetic plumbing that federation injects: a union enumerating every resolvable entity type, the two service-discovery root fields, the wrapper object type that carries the schema document, and the scalar types used by representations and key selections. Type, field and directive declarations are emitted in a normalized order.

feature3_entity_detection.json.description: Given a subgraph SDL, report which object types are resolvable federation entities, i.e. the members of the entity union exposed by the transformed schema. The output lists the total count followed by each entity type name in sorted order. Types that carry only a non-resolvable key are not entities and are excluded; a subgraph with no entity types yields a count of zero and no names.

cases.expected_output:
entity_count=5
entity=DeprecatedProduct
entity=Inventory
entity=Product
entity=ProductResearch
entity=User

feature4_invalid_schema_errors.json.description: Given a subgraph SDL that violates a federation composition rule, the transformation fails and the failure is surfaced as a neutral, language-independent error category line rather than a schema. Distinct categories distinguish: importing the federation specification at a version that is not supported, declaring the federation import link more than once, importing a feature from a specification version that predates it, attempting to rename a directive that may not be renamed, an entity that fails to declare the key required by an interface it implements, and generic schema-construction or schema-validation failures.
